transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.
I work on Month-end close. It is recurring, monthly, ~5 business days, and my role is that I owns the close; reviews and signs off, does not do most hands-on steps.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Sub-ledger cutoff; 2. Bank statement import & reconcile; 3. Journal review & approval; 4. Variance commentary; 5. Reporting pack & CFO sign-off.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Sub-ledger cutoff uses ERP with AP clerk and AR clerk; Bank statement import & reconcile uses ERP and recon tool and Excel with Staff accountant; Journal review & approval uses ERP with Staff accountant and Controller
What is most painful or slow today?
The annoying parts are mostly late vendor invoices push cutoff; manual matching of exceptions; judgment calls on accruals; waiting on budget-owner commentary; last-minute restatements.
What is most painful or slow today?
Staff accountant hands journals for approval to Controller; Budget owners hands variance commentary to FP&A analyst
I think I have enough to recap the workflow. Does this look right?

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.
I work on Invoice processing (AP). It is recurring, daily, continuous, and my role is that I does the hands-on processing end to end.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Receive invoices from shared mailbox; 2. PO match (3-way); 3. Clear exceptions; 4. Payment batch review; 5. Release for payment.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Receive invoices from shared mailbox uses shared mailbox and AP system with Vendors; PO match (3-way) uses AP system and ERP with Procurement; Clear exceptions uses AP system and email with Procurement and Requester
What is most painful or slow today?
The annoying parts are mostly invoices arrive in many formats; PO / receipt / invoice mismatches; chasing people for missing receipts; duplicate-invoice checks; cutoff timing for the payment run.
What is most painful or slow today?
AP Specialist hands payment batch for review to AP Manager; AP Manager hands approved batch for release to Treasury
I think I have enough to recap the workflow. Does this look right?

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.

I work on Client Q3 finance-transformation discovery. It is project, 12-week engagement, and my role is that I runs the engagement; the workflow being captured is the CLIENT finance team's close, not the consultant's own method.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Stakeholder interviews; 2. Current-state mapping; 3. Pain-point synthesis; 4. Opportunity sizing; 5. Roadmap & readout.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Stakeholder interviews uses interview guides and notes with Client finance leads; Current-state mapping uses whiteboard and slides with Client ops staff; Pain-point synthesis uses Excel and slides with Engagement team
What is most painful or slow today?
The annoying parts are mostly access to the right people; conflicting accounts of how work is done; separating symptoms from root causes; data to back the case; sponsor alignment on priorities.
What is most painful or slow today?
Engagement team hands synthesis and opportunity sizing to Client sponsor

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.

I work on Collections prioritization and dispute follow-up. It is recurring, weekly, with daily urgent checks, and my role is that I prioritizes overdue accounts and coordinates follow-up; does not approve write-offs.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Pull aging report; 2. Prioritize accounts; 3. Send follow-up and log responses; 4. Escalate disputes; 5. Prepare collections update.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Pull aging report uses ERP and Excel with AR analyst; Prioritize accounts uses Excel and collections notes with AR analyst and Sales; Send follow-up and log responses uses email and ERP with Customers and Sales
What is most painful or slow today?

The annoying parts are mostly data needs cleanup before review; unclear ownership on strategic accounts; responses are scattered across email threads; dispute reasons are inconsistent; manual status commentary.
What is most painful or slow today?
AR analyst hands customer follow-up requests to Sales; AR analyst hands dispute and escalation list to AR manager
I think I have enough to recap the workflow. Does this look right?

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.

I work on SOX control evidence collection. It is recurring, quarterly, with monthly control-owner reminders, and my role is that I coordinates evidence collection and reviews completeness; control owners supply evidence.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Send evidence request list; 2. Collect and log evidence; 3. Review evidence completeness; 4. Follow up on exceptions; 5. Prepare status summary.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Send evidence request list uses GRC tool and email with Control owners; Collect and log evidence uses GRC tool and document store with Control owners and SOX compliance officer; Review evidence completeness uses GRC tool and Excel with SOX compliance officer
What is most painful or slow today?

The annoying parts are mostly owners miss or misunderstand requests; file naming and version issues; support does not tie to the control period; open items linger near deadline; manual commentary on risk status.
What is most painful or slow today?
Control owners hands control evidence to SOX compliance officer; SOX compliance officer hands reviewed evidence and exception status to Internal audit

transcript.text: What task or workflow do you want to talk about? Briefly describe what happens, the business outcome, and the main output.

I work on Requirements intake and prioritization. It is project, each product increment, typically monthly, and my role is that I facilitates intake, documents requirements, and prepares prioritization inputs.
Where are the main handoffs or approvals?
At a high level the flow is 1. Collect change requests; 2. Clarify requirements; 3. Assess impact and dependencies; 4. Prepare prioritization pack.
What tools and data show up in the main steps?

Collect change requests uses intake form and ticket tracker with Business stakeholders; Clarify requirements uses workshops and notes with Business analyst and Product owner; Assess impact and dependencies uses ticket tracker and architecture notes with Engineering lead and Operations
What is most painful or slow today?
The annoying parts are mostly requests arrive with uneven detail; stakeholders describe solutions instead of needs; dependency information is scattered; manual roll-up of tradeoffs.
What is most painful or slow today?
Business stakeholders hands change requests to Business analyst; Business analyst hands prioritized requirement options to Product owner
